title: Basic Word Order in Uruangnirin
introduction: In many languages, the order of subject, verb, and object can vary. English normally follows Subject-Verb-Object (SVO): “She reads a book.” Uruangnirin, however, places the verb first and its object next (V O). If the subject is expressed as a free pronoun, it appears before the verb; otherwise, it is often omitted because Uruangnirin verbs carry subject markers. Possessors come before their nouns, while adjectives, numbers, and demonstratives follow the noun. Prepositions head their phrases and come before their complements. To express “be” in equative clauses, Uruangnirin uses the copula daka, which may appear before or after the noun phrase. Below are the key patterns with examples.

sections.focus: Basic clause word order: (S) V O

example.source_sentence: Well, I’ve met some of your relatives…
example.description: Transitive clause with verb lau (‘meet’) carrying the speaker-subject index, followed by the object NP omu nenang (‘your relatives’). V O order.

description.description: In Uruangnirin, ordinary clauses place the verb first, followed by the object. Subjects are often omitted because the verb carries a pronominal index.

example.source_sentence: He lives far away from here.
example.description: Overt subject pronoun ia (‘he’) precedes the verb amian (‘reside’), then the location PP olat nor dike (‘far from here’). S V PP order.

description.description: When the subject is a free pronoun, it appears before the verb; otherwise, subjects are dropped and indicated by the verb prefix.

example.description: The possessor pronoun omu (‘your’) precedes the head noun matananam (‘face’), followed by the adjective nakal (‘naughty’).

description.description: In Uruangnirin, possessors (pronouns or nouns) always precede the possessed noun.

example.target_sentence: kalo foto babair dike se daka
example.source_sentence: Who’s this on that first photo?
example.description: Head noun foto (‘photo’) + modifier babair (‘first’) + demonstrative dike (‘that’), question word kalo, wildcard se, and copula daka.

description.description: Adjectives, numerals, and demonstratives come after the noun they modify.

example.source_sentence: Did you search next to your bed?
example.description: Preposition amin (‘at’) precedes its object NP tompat rawawa som (‘your bed’).

description.description: Prepositional phrases are headed by a preposition placed before its object noun or phrase.

example.description: Copula daka appears before the noun phrase lanunggi popa (‘my uncle’) in an equative clause.

description.description: The equative “be” is expressed by daka, which can occur before or after the noun phrase.

conclusion:
What to remember:
- Main clauses: V O, with subjects omitted unless a free pronoun is used (then S V O).
- Possessor + noun: possessor precedes the noun.
- Noun‐modifier order: noun first, then adjectives, numbers, demonstratives.
- Prepositions come before their complements.
- Equative “be” is daka, placed either before or after the NP.

translation_drills.source: Well, I’ve met some of your relatives…
He lives far away from here.
Have you ever seen pictures of my family?

translation_drills.target: kalo foto babair dike se daka
translation_drills.source: Who’s this on that first photo?
Did you search next to your bed?
